titleTag: North Carolina Landlord Tenant Law 2026 | Rights, Deposits & Eviction
metaDescription: North Carolina landlord tenant law 2026: security deposit limits, eviction notice requirements, tenant rights & more. Plain-language guide for new landlords.

sections.heading: Security deposit rules
body: If you're looking for North Carolina security deposit rules, focus on (1) whether there is a cap, (2) when the deposit must be returned, and (3) whether an itemized statement is required.
bullets: Deposit cap: No statutory limit
Return deadline: 30 days after move-out/tenancy end (deductions can affect details).
Itemized statement: required if deductions are taken (check deadlines and delivery method).
Deductions: Varies — confirm current statute.
Penalty risk: Varies — confirm current statute.
Interest: Must pay interest on deposits held > 5 years (N.C.G.S. § 42-50)

sections.heading: Eviction notice requirements
body: For North Carolina eviction notice requirements, the notice period often depends on why the eviction is being pursued (nonpayment vs lease violation vs holdover). Always use the correct notice form and follow the court process.
bullets: Nonpayment notice: commonly 5 days (verify by tenancy type/notice form).
Nonpayment notice type: (1) Nonpayment of rent: 10-day demand for payment (N.C.G.S. §42-3). Tenant has 10 days from receipt to pay all past due rent before landlord can file for summary ejectment.
Lease violation notice: commonly 30 days (may allow cure).
Lease violation notice type: (2) Lease violation: 30-day written notice to cure or vacate (for most violations).
Cure allowed: varies
Month-to-month termination: commonly 30 days (verify tenancy type).
Holdover/termination notice type: (3) Month-to-month termination: 7-day notice prior to end of rental period.
Court process: varies — confirm court process requirements and exceptions.
Self-help eviction: prohibited (generally requires a court order).
Timeline:
Court steps: N.C.G.S. § 42-26. TENANT'S BREACH; TERMINATION BY LANDLORD.

sections.heading: Landlord entry notice
body: If you're searching for North Carolina landlord entry notice rules, look for (1) required notice time, (2) allowed entry reasons, and (3) emergency exceptions.
bullets: Notice: often governed by lease terms and a reasonableness standard (state law may not set a single hour-count).
Typical notice time: varies; many leases use 24 hours as a practical baseline.
Allowed reasons: Varies — commonly inspections, repairs, services, and showing the unit.
Timing: Varies — usually at reasonable times.
Emergencies: generally allow entry without advance notice.
Tenant refusal: tenant cannot unreasonably withhold consent
Remedies/penalties: Varies — confirm remedies (injunction, damages, attorney fees) under state law.

sections.heading: Rent increase rules
body: For North Carolina rent increase rules, most states focus on notice requirements, while rent caps are usually local (or limited to specific covered housing).
bullets: Statewide rent control: typically none, but local/covered-housing rules may apply.
Local rent control: may exist in certain cities/counties (check if your property is covered).
Notice of rent increase: commonly 7 days' written notice for month-to-month tenancies.
Fixed-term leases: Varies — confirm your lease terms and statute.
Month-to-month: Varies — generally allowed with proper notice.
Delivery: Varies — confirm written notice and delivery method requirements.
Just cause: varies
Retaliation: Retaliation protections may limit rent increases after protected tenant complaints; confirm local/state rules.

sections.heading: Tenant rights & habitability basics
body: Most North Carolina landlord tenant law disputes involve habitability, repairs, and retaliation protections. Keep repair requests documented and respond promptly.
bullets: Habitability: implied warranty generally applies
Landlord repair duty: (1) Comply with the current applicable building and housing codes, whether enacted before or after the effective date of this article;
Tenant notice: Varies — confirm required notice/cure steps before remedies.
Repair-and-deduct: varies
Rent withholding: varies
Termination: Varies — some states allow termination after notice and failure to repair.
Retaliation: varies
Code enforcement: Varies — contact local housing/code enforcement (city/county) for inspections and official repair orders.

jurisdictionNotes: Local ordinances (city/county) can be stricter than state law.
Rules can change; verify before acting on any single requirement.
confidenceNotes: This page is being refreshed for accuracy; key limits and notice periods are verified state-by-state during the rollout.
